note: Auto-generated from Notion vertigo.vault database. Do not edit directly.

activities.name: cheerleader
activities.headline: from rivals to raving fans
activities.content:
## **objective**: 
build energy and playful competition through escalating rounds of ro-sham-bo [rock-paper-scissors]
## **instructions**:
1. everyone starts by facing off with someone nearby in rock-paper-scissors.
1. whoever loses becomes the winner’s cheerleader: clapping, chanting their name, or doing silent gestures.
1. the winner moves on to battle another winner, now with a mini fan club.
1. **repeat **until there are only two final players, each with half the room cheering them on.
1. have the crowd cheer for their champion, and do one last epic battle on stage.

activities.name: game of assumptions
activities.headline: play with bias and gain insights
activities.content:
## **objective:**
use playful debate to explore how wrong assumptions can shape our beliefs, helping participants become more aware of the limits of their own knowledge and stay open to other perspectives.
## **instructions:**
1. **warm-up reflection**
1. **explain the activity**
1. **prepare your defense**
1. **play the game**
1. **group reflection**
1. **optional closing**
## 
assumptions you can use 
**additional to the ones in the wheel - we also encourage you to create your own!*
- a successful business leader prioritizes profits above all else
- ethical practices slow down business growth
- employees don’t care about sustainability
- sustainability only works in niche markets
- businesses can’t fully eliminate their carbon footprint
- greenwashing doesn’t really hurt a company
- consumers won’t pay more for sustainable products
- small companies can’t afford to be eco-friendly
- influencer campaigns are the best way to promote sustainability
- you can be both profitable and socially responsible

activities.name: mystery box
activities.headline: build stories from the unknown
activities.content:
## **objective:**
encourage co-creation and playful exploration of the unknown through story-building rooted in curiosity and imagination.
## **instructions:**
1. present the scene: a small, unlabeled box has appeared at your door. it has no weight… but it hums slightly. maybe it glows, whispers, or blinks.
1. turn to a partner or small group and ask: **what’s inside this box? who sent it and why?**
1. begin a story together, building it one sentence at a time.
1. each person takes a turn, adding a new line to continue the tale, reveal a clue, or introduce a twist.
## **optional twist:**
each group can read their favorite line aloud to the whole room. debrief by reflecting on:
- what surprised you in the story?
- what was it like to build something from uncertainty?
- how do different minds stretch a single prompt into wildly different possibilities?

activities.name: build a bridge
activities.headline: strengthen teams through creative engineering
activities.content:
## **objective:**
encourage participants to work together and share resources to achieve a common goal, while practicing communication and problem-solving in a team setting.
## **instructions:**
1. **set the scene**
1. **creation phase**
1. **testing the bridges**
1. **group reflection**

activities.name: ball relay
activities.headline: team coordination meets paper frenzy
activities.content:
## **objective:**
work together in lines to move a ball from the front to the back of the team using only paper—fast, fun, and full of teamwork chaos.
## **materials:**
1 lightweight ball per team (ping pong ball, crumpled paper, soft toy ball, etc.), 1 A4 (or letter = 8.5in x 11in) sheet of paper per person
## **instructions:**
1. form teams of 10–20 people and have each team stand in a single file line (front to back).
1. give each person one sheet of A4 (letter, 8.5x11) paper.
1. the person at the front starts with the ball on their paper.
1. the team’s task: 
1. first team to get their ball to the last person in line wins!

activities.name: solution mashup
activities.headline: fuse concepts to spark innovation
activities.content:
## **objective:**
encourage participants to combine unrelated business concepts and synthesize them into an innovative solution, fostering creativity and the ability to see connections between different ideas.
## **instructions:**
1. **set the scene**
1. **distribute concept cards**
1. **group ideation**
1. **present mashups**
1. **whole group debrief**
## **concept cards (print or slide-ready):**
• customer service
• sustainability
• corporate social responsibility (csr)
• remote work
• social media marketing
• brand loyalty
• e-commerce
• recycling programs
• subscription model
• fair trade practices
• influencer marketing
• crowdfunding
• carbon footprint reduction
• digital transformation
• ethical sourcing
• sustainable packaging
• circular economy
• supply chain transparency
• data privacy
• inclusive design
• zero-waste initiatives
• impact investing
• employee training and development
• affordable products for low-income consumers
• reusability
• freemium business models
• local community support
• green technology
• ethical consumerism

activities.name: soundscapes of the future
activities.headline: conduct tomorrow’s ideas through sound
activities.content:
## **objective**: 
co-create an auditory collage that represents visions of responsible futures.
## **Instructions**:
1. divide into sections of 20–30 people.
1. assign each group a theme (e.g., sustainability, justice, ethics, play, innovation).
1. ask them to create a sound or rhythm (claps, stomps, snaps, chants) that represents that theme.
1. conduct the whole group like an orchestra.

activities.name: whispers from another world
activities.headline: step into someone else’s imagination
activities.content:
## **objective:**
use curiosity to invent, explore, and inhabit imaginary worlds—strengthening empathy, perspective-shifting, and creative inquiry.
## **instructions:**
1. introduce the prompt: imagine you’ve just received a postcard from a world no one has ever heard of.
1. describe a made-up place, such as:
1. ask each person to take turns describing the place in more detail—its logic, beauty, rules, or daily life.
1. now, ask your buddy: **what question would you ask someone who lives there?**
1. once the question is asked, respond **in character**—as if you live in that world, using its unique logic, values, and voice.
## **optional twist:**
build on each other’s worlds, forming a connected galaxy of imagined realities. close by reflecting on:
- how it felt to explore a world through someone else’s imagination
- what surprised you
- what insights about real-world perspectives emerged through play

activities.name: fortune cookie
activities.headline: stretch your thinking with playful roles
activities.content:
## **objective:**
use role-play and imaginative thinking to explore each other’s real questions with empathy and fresh perspectives.
## **instructions:**
1. ask each participant to write down a real question they’re genuinely curious about. it can be deep, silly, or strange. here are some examples:
1. fold the paper like a fortune cookie and swap with a partner.
1. each person opens their “cookie” and chooses a **role **to respond from:
1. respond to your partner’s question from that role—playful, poetic, or professional is welcome.
1. take turns and invite light discussion about how the perspective shaped the answer.
## **debrief:**
reflect on what it felt like to stretch your mindset. what new possibilities emerged?
## optional: 
share one favorite response or unexpected insight with the full group.

activities.name: massive mingle
activities.headline: move, meet, share, repeat
activities.content:
## **objective**: 
rapidly form connections while discussing meaningful topics.
## **instructions**:
1. set upbeat music.
1. participants walk around freely. When the music stops, pair up and respond to a prompt.
1. prompts include:
1. repeat 3–4 times.

activities.name: curiosity undercover
activities.headline: uncover meaning and dig deeper
activities.content:
## **objective:**
strengthen your ability to stay curious longer—without jumping to conclusions or easy answers.
## **instructions:**
1. pair up participants and explain they are both **undercover agents **investigating a seemingly normal situation.
1. give them a simple everyday scene (e.g., someone watering a plant, a missing sock, a cat staring at a wall). their only investigative tool is the word **“why.”**
1. take turns asking only **“why” questions **that build on each other.
1. keep going until one of these natural endings occurs:
## debrief
reflecting on how it felt to follow curiosity without resolution.
## optional
share your favorite “why moment” with the group.

activities.name: world map
activities.headline: shape ideas into living models
activities.content:
## **objective;**
embody complex ideas in playful, physical forms.
## **instructions:**
1. ask people to walk around the room and then say a number to make groups; end with groups of 8-10 people. 
1. you will say a prompt for teams to create a living picture that embodies it. 
1. walk the “gallery”; invite a few to explain theirs.

activities.name: human spectrum
activities.headline: move your beliefs into the room
activities.content:
## **objective**:
Actively map the diversity of beliefs and practices in the room.
## **instructions**:
- designate a long line across the room (can be imaginary).
- participants move to where they sit with each prompt.
- **spectrum prompts:**

activities.name: story threads
activities.headline: weave metaphors into shared meaning
activities.content:
## **objective:**
reflect on personal strengths and roles through symbolic metaphors to open with warmth and connection.
## **instructions:**
1. form a large circle or online group.
1. invite each person to introduce themselves: **name, role, and what excites them most about their work**
1. prompt them to reflect on their **strongest contribution to a learning ecosystem**
1. share symbolic examples to inspire reflection:
1. ask each participant to select a metaphor that represents them.
1. in miro (or physically), have them **place their metaphor visually **in a shared space.
1. invite popcorn-style sharing to notice patterns and themes.
## **optional:**
close with a 2-minute self-assessment or reflective journaling on their metaphor and role.

activities.name: emoji charades
activities.headline: embody ideas with just faces
activities.content:
## **objective:**
act out emotions or abstract ideas in teams using only facial expressions and body language.
## **instructions:**
1. form teams of 10–15 people.
1. call out an emotion or concept (e.g., Imposter syndrome, Quarter-life crisis, Coffee addiction, Joy when WiFi works, Deadline panic).
1. teams have 30 seconds to create a frozen emoji tableau that captures the prompt.
1. on the count of 3: all teams reveal their poses. 
1. applaud the most creative or relatable ones.
## **optional twist**
have the crowd vote with applause or give fun awards (e.g., “Most Dramatic,” “Most Accurate”).

activities.name: character roll call
activities.headline: playful introduction to turn your voice up
activities.content:
## **objective:**
spark laughter and loosen up the room by encouraging playful self-expression and creative risk-taking.
## **instructions:**
1. prepare a slide or image board with a mix of well-known characters (e.g., mickey mouse, darth vader, shrek, moana, minion, yoda, elsa).
1. explain that each person will introduce themselves: **name, role, and one thing they’re curious about today**— but using the voice or style of one of the characters shown.
1. once a character voice has been used, it cannot be repeated.
1. go around the room or call on volunteers.
1. encourage playful commitment to the bit—silly, exaggerated, or just imaginative versions are welcome.
## **optional twist:**
after everyone has gone, invite a few to guess who was impersonating whom.

activities.name: question storm
activities.headline: fuel curiosity before finding answers
activities.content:
## **objective:**
quickly cultivate a questioning mindset by generating as many insightful questions as possible about a given business problem, encouraging deeper exploration before jumping to solutions.
## **instructions:**
1. **set the scene**
1. **question storm**
1. **review and highlight**
1. **share back**
## **sample prompts / business questions to explore:**
- how can businesses create an inclusive and diverse work environment?
- what strategies can companies use to attract and retain top talent in a competitive job market?
- how can businesses balance profitability with environmental sustainability?
- what role does social media play in shaping brand perception among consumers?
- how can companies foster innovation and creativity in their teams?
- how can businesses adapt to rapid technological change?
- how can we build customer loyalty without depending on discounts?
- how can businesses promote ethical practices across their supply chains?
- how can companies prepare for global economic uncertainty?
- how can businesses improve employee well-being and engagement?

activities.name: animal herding
activities.headline: non-verbal teamwork in motion
activities.content:
## **objective:**
Use movement and sound to group people without speaking.
## **instructions:**
1. assign an animal to everyone (e.g., dog, cat, elephant, penguin, frog)
1. when you say Go!, everyone must find their animal group: using only movement and sound (no words allowed).
1. once herds are formed, they must create and perform a 10-second group routine (a march, a cheer, or a synchronized movement).
## **optional:**
have a “zoo parade” where each herd performs for the rest of the group.

activities.name: how has play shaped you?
activities.headline: explore identity through playful reflection
activities.content:
## **objective:**
invite connection and reflection by linking personal hobbies to perceptions of play.
## **instructions:**
1. form a large circle or online group.
1. invite each participant to introduce themselves: **name, role, organization, and one hobby that has reshaped the way they think about play**
1. encourage brief, focused sharing to keep energy moving.
1. as themes emerge, lightly highlight interesting connections between hobbies and professional perspectives.
## **optional:**
note common threads or surprising contrasts on a board or shared doc to revisit later.

activities.name: swap perspectives
activities.headline: see decisions from all angles
activities.content:
## **objective:**
encourage participants to consider different viewpoints by stepping into the shoes of various stakeholders affected by a real-world business decision.
## **instructions:**
1. **set the scene**
1. **distribute roles**
1. **small group discussion**
1. **whole group debrief**
1. **optional twist:**
## **role cards (print or copy into slides):**
### **ceo**
“you’re proud of the company’s expansion—this new headquarters represents innovation, growth, and long-term success. you believe this move was necessary to stay competitive and offer better working conditions. you’re aware of some community backlash, but think the overall benefits outweigh the costs.”
### **local business owner**
“you ran events and networked with other small entrepreneurs at the now-demolished community center. it was a vital space for your business and others like you. you’re frustrated that a valuable public space was removed without real consultation.”
### **employee**
“you love the company and feel proud to work there. but the new location means a longer commute, and you’re unsure whether the company’s sustainability promises are still being prioritized with this rapid expansion.”
### **sustainability officer**
“you’re responsible for keeping the company’s environmental practices strong, but the fast pace of expansion makes it hard. you’re feeling pressure to greenlight things quickly and worry about the long-term impact on your credibility and the planet.”
### l**ocal resident**
“you’ve lived near the community center for years. it was a place where your kids played, where neighbors gathered, where the community felt alive. now it’s a big building you didn’t ask for, and you’re worried it’ll increase traffic and make your neighborhood less affordable.”
### **tech investor**
“you’re thrilled. the expansion signals growth and higher profits. you believe in the leadership and want them to move quickly and capitalize on this momentum. environmental concerns are valid but shouldn’t slow things down too much.”
### **city official**
“you helped approve the new headquarters. you hoped it would bring jobs and prestige to the city—but you’re hearing from locals who feel displaced and ignored. you’re caught between growth and inclusion, and it’s weighing on you.”
### **environmental activist**
“you’ve protested the headquarters project. to you, it represents another example of corporate greenwashing. the company says it’s sustainable, but they replaced public space with more concrete and energy use. you believe cities should prioritize nature and community.”

activities.name: memory game
activities.headline: challenge your brain with rapid recall
activities.content:
## **objective:**
practice working memory with a sequence of number and color combinations.
## **instructions:**
1. share a combination of numbers on a screen
1. explain participants that you will share, then hide and then they have to remember and share the sequence (in sli.do or mentimeter) 
1. increase difficulty by increasing numbers, then colors, then colors + numbers.

activities.name: mirror match
activities.headline: find your twin without words
activities.content:
## **objective**: 
warm up the group and spark playfulness by tapping into memory, movement, and shared meaning.
## **instructions**:
1. explain the game as an online version of the card game memory but with people instead of cards.
1. turn cameras on.
1. each round, the facilitator privately assigns matching pairs a shared identity (sent via chat or DM):
1. all participants act out their identity with movement or sound (no words).
1. while everyone is acting, players try to find their matching “card” by observing and guessing.
1. when someone thinks they’ve found their match, they name the person and what they are.
1. facilitator confirms if it’s a match. If wrong, keep playing.
1. continue until all matches are found.
1. **optional twist: **final round where participants create their own matching categories

activities.name: the fishbowl conflict
activities.headline: watch a live difficult conversation unfold, then coach the participants
activities.content:
## **objective:**
develop conflict observation and coaching skills by watching a live difficult conversation, analysing the dynamics, and offering targeted feedback.
## **instructions:**
1. **setup:** arrange chairs in two circles:
1. **brief the inner circle** with a conflict scenario:
1. **round 1 — the conversation (7 min):**
1. **pause and coach (8 min):**
1. **round 2 — the redo (5 min):**
## **debrief questions:**
- what patterns did observers see that participants didn't feel?
- what was the turning point in the conversation?
- what one skill would have changed the outcome most?

activities.name: silent sort
activities.headline: organize yourselves without a single word
activities.content:
## **objective:**
practice non-verbal communication and collaborative problem-solving by arranging the group into a specific order without speaking.
## **instructions:**
1. **set up**
1. **round 1 — birthday order**
1. **round 2 — pick a harder sort** (optional)
1. **debrief**

activities.name: innovation theatre
activities.headline: act out your product pitch as a 60-second infomercial
activities.content:
## **objective:**
transform a product or service concept into a wildly entertaining 60-second infomercial, forcing teams to distil their idea to its emotional core.
## **instructions:**
1. each team selects (or is assigned) an innovation concept they've been developing.
1. **the brief:** create a 60-second TV infomercial for your concept. it must include:
1. teams have **10 minutes** to prepare.
1. perform live for the group. ham it up.
1. audience votes on: most convincing, funniest, and most likely to actually work.
## **materials:**
- any props available in the room
- phones for recording (optional)
## **debrief questions:**
- what did the infomercial format force you to clarify about your idea?
- which pitch actually made you want to buy the product?
- how does storytelling change the way an idea lands?

activities.name: the boundary-setting workshop
activities.headline: practise saying no clearly, kindly, and without guilt
activities.content:
## **objective:**
develop the skill of setting clear, respectful boundaries in professional relationships, including handling pushback without caving or escalating.
## **instructions:**
1. introduce the **boundary formula:**
1. **practice tier 1 — easy boundaries (5 min):**
1. **practice tier 2 — medium boundaries (5 min):**
1. **practice tier 3 — hard boundaries + pushback (10 min):**
## **the broken record technique:**
when someone pushes back, calmly repeat your boundary in slightly different words without escalating:
- "I understand, and I'm still not able to take this on."
- "I hear that it's important, and my answer is still no."
- "I appreciate you asking, and this isn't something I can do right now."
## **debrief questions:**
- what makes saying no feel so difficult?
- what's the cost of not setting boundaries?
- which tier was the tipping point for you?

activities.name: the elephant naming ceremony
activities.headline: collectively name and address the issues everyone knows about but nobody discusses
activities.content:
## **objective:**
create a structured, safe process for naming the undiscussable issues in a team or organisation — the things everyone knows about but nobody talks about.
## **instructions:**
1. **set the container (5 min):**
1. **silent elephant writing (5 min):**
1. **the naming ceremony (10 min):**
1. **acknowledgement round (5 min):**
1. **action selection (5 min):**
## **materials:**
- blank cards or sticky notes
- pens
- wall space
## **debrief questions:**
- how did it feel to see your elephant read aloud?
- why do certain topics become undiscussable?
- what needs to be true for us to address elephants routinely?

activities.name: prototype in 5
activities.headline: build a rough prototype in 5 minutes using only what's in the room
activities.content:
## **objective:**
practice rapid prototyping by building a physical or visual representation of an idea using only materials immediately available.
## **instructions:**
1. give each team a challenge statement (e.g., "design a better waiting room experience" or "create a tool for giving feedback").
1. set the constraint: **you can only use materials already in this room** — paper, pens, cups, phones, bags, furniture, people.
1. teams have exactly **5 minutes** to build something that represents their solution.
1. each team gets **60 seconds** to demo their prototype.
1. audience gives feedback using "I like / I wish / what if" format.
## **materials:**
whatever is already in the room — that's the point.
## **debrief questions:**
- what did the time constraint force you to do differently?
- how did the material limitation spark creativity?
- what surprised you about another team's approach?

activities.name: idea speed dating
activities.headline: pair up, pitch, rotate — rapid-fire idea cross-pollination
activities.content:
## **objective:**
generate a high volume of novel ideas by rapidly sharing, building on, and combining concepts across rotating pairs.
## **instructions:**
1. each participant writes one idea on a sticky note — just a seed, not a full plan.
1. form two lines facing each other (or use breakout rooms online).
1. pairs have **90 seconds** each:
1. write the mashup on a fresh sticky note.
1. one line rotates. repeat for 4-5 rounds.
1. at the end, each person chooses their favourite mashup to share with the group.
## **debrief questions:**
- which combinations surprised you?
- did your original idea evolve through the rounds?
- what made certain pairings click?
## **twists:**
- **constraint round**: add a random constraint card before each rotation (e.g., "make it work underwater" or "budget of €10")
- **silent version**: no talking — communicate ideas only through sketches

activities.name: two truths and a sketch
activities.headline: draw your way into knowing each other
activities.content:
## **objective:**
build connection through playful drawing — a variation on the classic icebreaker that replaces words with pictures, making it more accessible and surprising.
## **instructions:**
1. **explain the twist**
1. **drawing phase (5 min)**
1. **guessing phase (8 min)**
1. **debrief (2 min)**
## **materials:**
- paper (1 sheet per person or 3 sticky notes each)
- pens or markers

activities.name: dilemma dash
activities.headline: tackle ethical puzzles under time pressure
activities.content:
## **objective:**
practice ethical reasoning and perspective-taking by working through real-world dilemmas quickly, noticing how time pressure affects the quality of moral thinking.
## **instructions:**
1. **set the rules**
1. **round 1 (3 min)**
1. **round 2 (3 min)**
1. **round 3 — slow round (5 min)**
1. **whole group debrief (5 min)**
## **example dilemma cards:**
- your company discovers a supplier uses child labor. pulling the contract would cost 200 jobs at your company. what do you do?
- you find a colleague has been inflating sales numbers. they're also the sole breadwinner for their family. do you report it?
- a community garden is thriving but built on land a developer just bought. they offer to relocate it to a less convenient spot. do you accept?
- your team has budget to fund one project: clean water for 500 people or education materials for 2,000 children. which do you choose?
## **materials:**
- printed or handwritten dilemma cards
- paper and pens for notes
- timer

activities.name: random input
activities.headline: force unexpected connections by linking your challenge to a random object
activities.content:
## **objective:**
break out of predictable thinking patterns by forcing associations between your challenge and an unrelated random stimulus.
## **instructions:**
1. state the challenge or problem clearly.
1. introduce a **random input** — use one of these methods:
1. write the random word/object where everyone can see it.
1. set a timer for **5 minutes**.
1. generate as many connections as possible between the random input and your challenge:
1. circle the 2-3 most interesting connections and develop them further.
## **example:**
challenge: "how might we improve employee onboarding?"
random input: "umbrella"
connections: protection from overwhelm, opens and closes (phased introduction), portable (mobile-first), shared (buddy system), colourful (personalised).
## **debrief questions:**
- which connection surprised you most?
- how did the constraint of the random word force a different direction?
- could you use this technique solo?

activities.name: the power dynamics map
activities.headline: make invisible power structures visible so the group can navigate them honestly
activities.content:
## **objective:**
make visible the formal and informal power dynamics within a team or organisation, enabling more honest and equitable communication.
## **instructions:**
1. explain the concept: "every group has power dynamics — they affect who speaks, who's heard, whose ideas get adopted, and who feels safe to disagree. ignoring them doesn't make them go away."
1. **individual mapping (5 min):** each person draws a map of power in their team/organisation:
1. **compare maps (8 min):**
1. **impact discussion (7 min):**
1. **commitment (3 min):**
## **debrief questions:**
- did people with more power and less power see the map differently?
- what's the difference between having power and abusing it?
- how does making power visible change how you communicate?

activities.name: assumption smash
activities.headline: surface and challenge the hidden assumptions blocking innovation
activities.content:
## **objective:**
identify and systematically challenge the assumptions underlying a product, process, or problem to unlock new possibilities.
## **instructions:**
1. choose a topic — a product, service, process, or industry norm.
1. **round 1 — surface assumptions (5 min):**
1. **round 2 — sort and select (3 min):**
1. **round 3 — smash (10 min):**
1. share the most provocative "smashed" idea from each group.
## **example:**
assumption: "restaurants need physical menus"
smashed: what if diners described what they're craving and the chef improvised?
## **debrief questions:**
- which assumption was hardest to challenge? why?
- did smashing an assumption reveal a real opportunity?
- where in your work are you operating on unexamined assumptions?

activities.name: the repair conversation
activities.headline: practise rebuilding trust after it's been broken
activities.content:
## **objective:**
learn a structured approach to rebuilding trust after a breach, going beyond apology to create a concrete repair plan that both parties can commit to.
## **instructions:**
1. introduce the **trust repair framework:**
1. distribute **trust breach scenario cards:**
1. **practice in pairs (12 min):**
1. **coaching and redo (8 min):**
## **debrief questions:**
- which step of the framework was hardest to execute?
- what's the difference between an apology and a repair?
- what makes some trust breaches harder to repair than others?

activities.name: the pivot game
activities.headline: your original idea just failed — pivot in 3 minutes or you're out
activities.content:
## **objective:**
practice rapid strategic pivoting by responding to unexpected market feedback, building adaptability and creative resilience.
## **instructions:**
1. each team starts with a product or business concept (use existing ideas or assign new ones).
1. teams have **3 minutes** to prepare a quick pitch for their concept.
1. after pitching, the facilitator delivers a **pivot trigger** — a scenario that makes the original concept unviable:
1. teams have exactly **3 minutes** to pivot: change the target market, the business model, the core feature, or all three.
1. pitch the pivot. facilitator delivers another trigger.
1. repeat for **3 rounds** of pivots.
1. final pitch: present the version that survived all pivots.
## **debrief questions:**
- how did your idea evolve through forced pivots?
- which pivot trigger was hardest to respond to?
- did any pivot actually make the idea stronger?
- what does this tell us about rigidity vs. flexibility in innovation?

activities.name: the question wall
activities.headline: anonymously surface the questions everyone is thinking but nobody is asking
activities.content:
## **objective:**
create psychological safety by separating difficult questions from the people asking them, surfacing unspoken concerns and taboo topics.
## **instructions:**
1. give everyone **3 sticky notes** and a pen.
1. set the prompt: "write the questions you've been wanting to ask but haven't. the things you're wondering about but feel awkward raising."
1. collect all sticky notes face-down. shuffle them.
1. post them randomly on a wall or board.
1. **silent reading (2 min):** everyone reads all the questions.
1. **dot vote:** give each person 3 dots. vote on the questions you most want answered or discussed.
1. take the top 3-5 voted questions and discuss them as a group.
## **ground rules:**
- no guessing who wrote what
- no dismissing any question as "silly"
- it's okay to say "I don't know the answer yet"
## **debrief questions:**
- what themes emerged from the questions?
- were you surprised by what others were wondering about?
- how could we make it easier to ask these questions regularly?

activities.name: innovation auction
activities.headline: bid on ideas with limited resources to reveal what your team truly values
activities.content:
## **objective:**
use a simulated auction to force prioritisation of ideas and surface the group's real innovation preferences.
## **instructions:**
1. **generate ideas (5 min):** brainstorm 10-15 innovation ideas related to a shared challenge. write each on a card and post them where everyone can see.
1. **distribute currency:** give each participant **€100 in play money** (use paper slips in denominations of €10, €20, €50).
1. **set auction rules:**
1. **run the auction (10 min):** auction all ideas. keep it fast and energetic.
1. **debrief (5 min):**
## **materials:**
- idea cards
- play money (paper slips work fine)
- a gavel or noisemaker for the facilitator (optional but fun)
## **twist:**
- **secret investor:** one person gets €200 but can't reveal it until the auction starts

activities.name: active listening triads
activities.headline: practise listening to understand, not to respond
activities.content:
## **objective:**
experience the difference between listening to respond and listening to understand by practising structured active listening with a dedicated observer.
## **instructions:**
1. form groups of 3. assign roles:
1. **round 1 (4 min):**
1. **observer feedback (1 min):** the observer shares what they noticed — where the listener was effective and where they defaulted to fixing mode.
1. **rotate roles** and repeat for 2 more rounds so everyone experiences all 3 roles.
## **debrief questions:**
- which role was hardest? why?
- what happened when the listener truly just listened?
- what's the difference between being heard and being advised?

activities.name: design thinking sprint
activities.headline: empathize, define, ideate, prototype — in 30 minutes
activities.content:
## **objective:**
experience the full design thinking cycle in a compressed format, building empathy-driven solutions to real human problems through rapid iteration.
## **instructions:**
1. **empathy interviews (5 min)**
1. **define the problem (3 min)**
1. **ideate (5 min)**
1. **prototype (10 min)**
1. **share and test (5 min)**
1. **debrief (2 min)**
## **materials:**
- paper and pens
- sticky notes
- markers
- timer
- any available craft materials for prototyping

activities.name: feedback compass
activities.headline: give and receive feedback using the four cardinal directions
activities.content:
## **objective:**
practise giving balanced, actionable feedback using a structured framework that goes beyond simple positive/negative categories.
## **instructions:**
1. draw a compass on paper or whiteboard with 4 directions:
1. choose a feedback target — this could be:
1. **silent writing (5 min):** each person writes feedback on sticky notes, one per compass direction.
1. **share and discuss (8 min):**
1. the recipient identifies **one thing from each direction** they'll act on.
## **ground rules:**
- be specific ("your opening slide grabbed attention" not "nice presentation")
- focus on behaviour, not character
- "south" feedback is about improvement, not criticism
## **debrief questions:**
- which direction was easiest to give feedback in? hardest?
- how did it feel to receive feedback without responding?
- how is this different from typical feedback conversations?

activities.name: the 3-sentence story
activities.headline: tell a conflict story in exactly 3 sentences to practise clarity and ownership
activities.content:
## **objective:**
practise distilling a conflict or difficult situation into its essential elements, building clarity and personal accountability in how we narrate disagreements.
## **instructions:**
1. each person thinks of a **recent difficult interaction** — a disagreement, a frustrating conversation, a moment of tension.
1. write it as a **3-sentence story** using this structure:
1. example:
1. share stories in pairs. the listener's only job is to **reflect back** what they heard.
1. after hearing the reflection, the speaker can revise their 3 sentences if the reflection revealed something.
## **why it works:**
most conflict stories are long, tangled, and full of blame. the 3-sentence constraint forces ownership ("I felt" not "they made me feel") and forward focus ("I need" not "they should").
## **debrief questions:**
- what was hardest to write — the facts, the feeling, or the need?
- how did hearing it reflected back change your understanding?
- could you use this format before a real difficult conversation?

activities.name: constraint bingo
activities.headline: fill your bingo card with creative solutions under escalating constraints
activities.content:
## **objective:**
practice creative problem-solving under increasingly tight constraints while maintaining energy and momentum.
## **instructions:**
1. give each participant a **3×3 bingo card** (9 squares).
1. present a design challenge (e.g., "design a better commute" or "reimagine the lunch break").
1. reveal constraints one at a time. after each constraint is revealed:
1. **example constraint sequence:**
1. when someone fills a row, column, or diagonal — they shout "bingo!"
1. bingo winners share their solutions. group votes on the most creative.
## **materials:**
- printed or drawn 3×3 bingo cards
- pens
- prepared constraint cards
## **debrief questions:**
- did the constraints help or hinder your creativity?
- which constraint was the tipping point?
- how does this relate to real-world innovation under pressure?

activities.name: stakeholder gallery walk
activities.headline: map who’s affected and what they’d say
activities.content:
## **objective:**
develop systems thinking and empathy by mapping the perspectives of multiple stakeholders affected by a real-world issue, then physically engaging with each perspective through a gallery walk format.
## **instructions:**
1. **choose the issue**
1. **identify stakeholders (5 min)**
1. **stakeholder deep dive (10 min)**
1. **gallery walk (5 min)**
1. **synthesis and debrief (8 min)**
## **materials:**
- large paper (1 sheet per stakeholder)
- markers
- sticky notes
- pens

activities.name: temperature check
activities.headline: use a physical scale to surface how people really feel about an issue
activities.content:
## **objective:**
create a safe, low-verbal way for participants to express their emotional state or stance on a topic, making invisible group dynamics visible.
## **instructions:**
1. clear a space in the room. designate one end as **"completely comfortable"** and the other as **"deeply uncomfortable."**
1. state a topic, question, or scenario. examples:
1. ask everyone to **stand on the spectrum** — no talking yet.
1. once positioned, look around. notice the distribution.
1. **invite sharing (optional):** ask 2-3 people at different points to briefly explain their position.
1. repeat with 2-3 different questions.
## **why it works:**
standing somewhere is easier than speaking up. the physical movement creates commitment without requiring vulnerability through words first. the visual distribution often surprises the group.
## **debrief questions:**
- were you surprised by where others stood?
- did anyone want to move after hearing others' explanations?
- what does the distribution tell us about our team?

activities.name: idea relay
activities.headline: pass ideas around the circle — each person builds on what came before
activities.content:
## **objective:**
build increasingly refined ideas through sequential collaboration, where each person contributes one layer before passing it on.
## **instructions:**
1. each person starts with a blank sheet of paper and writes a **problem statement** at the top.
1. pass the paper to the left.
1. **round 1 — seed (2 min):** read the problem. write one idea to address it. fold the paper to hide the problem statement. pass left.
1. **round 2 — build (2 min):** read the previous person's idea. add a detail, improvement, or twist. fold to hide the original idea. pass left.
1. **round 3 — challenge (2 min):** read what's visible. write one reason it might fail and one way to fix it. pass left.
1. **round 4 — name it (1 min):** read the full chain. give the concept a catchy name and a one-line pitch.
1. unfold all papers. read the full evolution from problem → idea → build → challenge → pitch.
1. each person presents the final concept they're holding (not the one they started).
## **debrief questions:**
- how did your original idea change through the relay?
- which round added the most value?
- what does this tell us about collaborative vs. solo ideation?

activities.name: the values collision
activities.headline: navigate a scenario where two deeply held values are in direct conflict
activities.content:
## **objective:**
explore the complexity of ethical decision-making by navigating scenarios where two legitimate values are in direct opposition, building tolerance for moral ambiguity.
## **instructions:**
1. introduce **values collisions** — situations where two good things conflict:
1. **scenario round (5 min):** present a detailed scenario involving a values collision. give enough context to make both sides genuinely compelling.
1. **individual reflection (3 min):** each person writes:
1. **group discussion (10 min):**
1. **meta-reflection (5 min):**
## **debrief questions:**
- was there a clear right answer? why or why not?
- what makes values collisions harder than factual disagreements?
- how do you work with someone whose value hierarchy differs from yours?

activities.name: innovation safari
activities.headline: hunt for real-world innovations hiding in plain sight around your environment
activities.content:
## **objective:**
sharpen observational skills by finding examples of clever design, innovation, and problem-solving in your immediate surroundings.
## **instructions:**
1. form teams of 3-4 people.
1. give each team a **safari checklist** with categories:
1. teams have **12 minutes** to explore the building, street, or campus.
1. document findings with phone photos or quick sketches.
1. return and present your top 3 finds — explain:
## **materials:**
- printed safari checklists (or write categories on a whiteboard to photograph)
- phones for documenting
## **debrief questions:**
- what was the most surprising innovation you found?
- which category was hardest to fill?
- how might you apply "safari thinking" to your next project?
## **twist:**
- **reverse safari:** find things that are terribly designed — then redesign them in 3 minutes

activities.name: worst idea first
activities.headline: deliberately generate terrible ideas to unlock creative thinking
activities.content:
## **objective:**
bypass the inner critic by intentionally brainstorming the worst possible solutions, then mining them for hidden brilliance.
## **instructions:**
1. present the group with a real challenge or design question.
1. set the rule: **only terrible ideas allowed.** the worse, the better.
1. spend **5 minutes** generating as many awful solutions as possible.
1. cluster the bad ideas on a wall or board.
1. now flip: pick 3 of the worst ideas and ask:
1. develop at least one "rescued" idea into a real concept.
## **why it works:**
fear of judgement is the biggest killer of creative ideas. by making bad ideas the goal, you remove that barrier entirely. paradoxically, some of the best innovations started as jokes.
## **debrief questions:**
- how did it feel to be told your ideas should be bad?
- which "terrible" idea had the most hidden potential?
- how might you use this technique in your real work?

activities.name: red line / green line
activities.headline: map the behaviours that build trust and the ones that destroy it
activities.content:
## **objective:**
collaboratively define the specific behaviours that build psychological safety (green line) and those that erode it (red line) within your team.
## **instructions:**
1. draw a horizontal line across a large piece of paper or whiteboard. label it:
1. give everyone sticky notes in two colours (or use markers).
1. **silent brainstorm (5 min):** each person writes specific, observable behaviours:
1. post all notes. read through them as a group.
1. **discussion (5 min):**
1. optionally, select **3 green commitments** the team will actively practise.
## **debrief questions:**
- was anything on the red line that you didn't realise was damaging?
- how do you call out a red-line behaviour without creating another red-line moment?
- what would change if we consistently stayed above the green line?

activities.name: reverse brainstorm
activities.headline: make the problem worse to find the real solution
activities.content:
## **objective:**
unlock creative solutions by first imagining how to make a problem as bad as possible — then flipping those ideas into actionable improvements.
## **instructions:**
1. **introduce the method**
1. **choose a problem (2 min)**
1. **reverse brainstorm (5 min)**
1. **flip phase (5 min)**
1. **share and debrief (5 min)**
## **materials:**
- sticky notes
- markers
- flipchart or wall space

activities.name: conflict style shuffle
activities.headline: discover your default conflict style and learn when to switch
activities.content:
## **objective:**
help participants identify their default conflict-handling style and understand when different styles are appropriate.
## **instructions:**
1. post **5 signs** around the room, each labelled with a conflict style:
1. read a conflict scenario aloud. examples:
1. everyone walks to the sign that represents how they'd **actually** respond (not how they'd ideally respond).
1. once positioned, each group briefly discusses:
1. repeat with 3-4 different scenarios. notice if people move between styles.
## **debrief questions:**
- did you stay in the same spot or move across scenarios?
- which style is your default? is that serving you?
- when is each style the right choice?

activities.name: the accountability circle
activities.headline: practise holding someone accountable without damaging the relationship
activities.content:
## **objective:**
develop the skill of holding others accountable in a way that addresses behaviour, preserves dignity, and strengthens rather than damages relationships.
## **instructions:**
1. introduce the **accountability framework:**
1. distribute **scenario cards** with accountability situations:
1. **practice round 1 (5 min per pair):**
1. **coaching pause:** observer shares feedback. what was clear? what got lost? where did the speaker hedge or over-explain?
1. **practice round 2:** same scenario, improved delivery.
1. **reverse perspective (5 min):** now practise being the one held accountable:
## **debrief questions:**
- what's the difference between accountability and criticism?
- why do we tend to avoid these conversations?
- what makes accountability feel caring rather than punishing?

activities.name: feelings inventory
activities.headline: name the emotion before naming the problem
activities.content:
## **objective:**
practice emotional literacy by learning to accurately identify and name emotions before jumping to problem-solving.
## **instructions:**
1. distribute a **feelings word list** — a sheet with 40+ emotion words grouped by category:
1. present a scenario relevant to the group (e.g., "a project you worked hard on gets deprioritised").
1. each person silently selects **2-3 words** from the list that best capture how they'd feel.
1. go around the circle. each person shares their words — just the words, no explanation yet.
1. notice: people chose different words for the same scenario.
1. now invite anyone to explain their choice: "I chose 'invisible' because..."
1. repeat with 1-2 more scenarios.
## **why it works:**
most people default to "frustrated" or "fine" when asked how they feel. a richer vocabulary for emotions leads to more precise communication, better self-awareness, and more productive difficult conversations.
## **debrief questions:**
- was it easy or hard to find the right words?
- did hearing others' choices change how you understood the scenario?
- when might naming the emotion prevent a conflict from escalating?

activities.name: innovation autopsy
activities.headline: dissect a famous failure to extract lessons for your own project
activities.content:
## **objective:**
learn from real-world innovation failures by conducting a structured post-mortem, then apply those lessons to your own work.
## **instructions:**
1. deal each team a **failure case study card** — a famous product or company that failed:
1. **autopsy phase (12 min):** analyse the failure using this framework:
1. **presentation (2 min per team):** present your autopsy findings.
1. **transfer phase (5 min):**
## **materials:**
- prepared failure case study cards (3-4 paragraphs of context each)
- whiteboard or large paper for autopsy framework
## **debrief questions:**
- was there a failure pattern common across multiple cases?
- what's the difference between a failed innovation and a bad idea?
- how do you create a culture where early warning signs get heard?

activities.name: cross-industry steal
activities.headline: borrow a solution from an unrelated industry and apply it to yours
activities.content:
## **objective:**
break industry tunnel vision by systematically borrowing strategies, processes, or business models from unrelated sectors.
## **instructions:**
1. define your challenge clearly (e.g., "how might we improve customer retention?").
1. deal each team **2 industry cards** from a shuffled deck — industries completely unrelated to their own (e.g., Formula 1 racing, hospital emergency rooms, dating apps, theme parks).
1. **research phase (8 min):** teams spend time identifying how their assigned industries solve problems similar to their challenge.
1. **transfer phase (10 min):** map at least 3 strategies from the assigned industries onto your own challenge.
1. **pitch (2 min per team):** present your best cross-industry steal.
## **materials:**
- industry cards (prepare 20+ diverse industries on index cards)
- phones/laptops for quick research
- whiteboard or large paper
## **debrief questions:**
- which industry offered the most surprising parallel?
- why do we tend to only look within our own sector for solutions?
- which cross-industry steal could you actually implement?

activities.name: future headlines
activities.headline: write tomorrow's newspaper headline about your project's success
activities.content:
## **objective:**
use backcasting to envision success and work backwards to identify the steps needed to get there.
## **instructions:**
1. set the scene: "it's 2 years from now. your project has been wildly successful. a journalist is writing about it."
1. each team writes:
1. teams have **8 minutes** to craft their articles.
1. each team presents their headline to the group as if it's a real news broadcast.
1. after all presentations, discuss:
## **twists:**
- **scandal edition:** write the headline if everything went wrong — what does that reveal about risks?
- **dual timeline:** write both the success headline and the "what could go wrong" headline
## **debrief questions:**
- how does starting from the end change your thinking?
- which elements of the headline feel achievable right now?
- what gap exists between today and the headline?

activities.name: stakeholder empathy map
activities.headline: map what your users think, feel, say, and do to design solutions that actually fit
activities.content:
## **objective:**
develop deep user empathy by mapping the cognitive and emotional landscape of your target user, creating a foundation for human-centred innovation.
## **instructions:**
1. identify your target user or stakeholder.
1. draw a large **empathy map** divided into 4 quadrants:
1. **fill the map (10 min):**
1. **find the tensions (5 min):**
1. **create a persona (5 min):**
1. present your persona and their key tension to the group.
## **materials:**
- large paper or whiteboard
- sticky notes (4 colours if possible — one per quadrant)
- markers
## **debrief questions:**
- what surprised you about the contradictions?
- how might you validate your assumptions?
- how does this change what you'd build for this person?

activities.name: perspective swap
activities.headline: argue the opposite side of a disagreement you actually care about
activities.content:
## **objective:**
build empathy and reduce polarisation by requiring participants to genuinely argue a position they disagree with.
## **instructions:**
1. identify a topic the group has genuine disagreement about. it could be:
1. pair people up. each person shares their **actual position** on the topic (1 min each).
1. now **swap:** each person must argue their partner's position as convincingly as possible.
1. each person delivers their "swapped" argument (2 min each).
1. after both have spoken, discuss:
## **debrief questions:**
- what was hardest about arguing the other side?
- did you discover any merit in the opposing view?
- how might this change how you approach disagreements?

activities.name: minimum viable experiment
activities.headline: design the cheapest, fastest way to test whether your idea has legs
activities.content:
## **objective:**
learn the discipline of lean experimentation by designing the smallest possible test to validate (or kill) an innovation idea.
## **instructions:**
1. each team starts with an innovation idea they want to test.
1. identify the **riskiest assumption** — the one thing that, if wrong, kills the idea.
1. design a **minimum viable experiment** using this template:
1. **experiment types to consider:**
1. present your MVE to the group for feedback.
1. group votes: would you trust this experiment's results?
## **materials:**
- MVE template (printed or projected)
- sticky notes for hypothesis crafting
- whiteboard
## **debrief questions:**
- what's the difference between an experiment and a pilot?
- how do you resist the urge to build before testing?
- what would you do if the experiment results were ambiguous?

activities.name: innovation risk matrix
activities.headline: map your ideas on a risk-reward grid to make braver decisions
activities.content:
## **objective:**
evaluate and prioritise innovation ideas using a structured risk-reward framework that encourages bold thinking over safe bets.
## **instructions:**
1. draw a large **2x2 matrix** on a whiteboard or flipchart:
1. gather all current innovation ideas on sticky notes (one idea per note).
1. **placement round (10 min):**
1. **analysis round (10 min):**
1. for each idea in the top-right quadrant, ask:
1. commit to **one bold bet** from the top-right quadrant.
## **materials:**
- flipchart or whiteboard
- sticky notes
- markers
## **debrief questions:**
- did any idea change quadrants during discussion?
- what makes something feel "risky" — is it the idea or our comfort zone?
- why do organisations tend to avoid the top-right quadrant?

activities.name: reverse pitch
activities.headline: pitch your competitor's product — then find what yours does better
activities.content:
## **objective:**
deepen competitive awareness by genuinely advocating for a competitor's product, then identifying authentic differentiators for your own.
## **instructions:**
1. assign each team a competitor (real or hypothetical).
1. **phase 1 — become the competitor (8 min):**
1. **phase 2 — pitch the competition (2 min per team):**
1. **phase 3 — find your edge (5 min):**
1. present your competitive edge in **60 seconds**.
## **debrief questions:**
- what was it like to genuinely praise a competitor?
- did understanding their strengths change how you see your own product?
- which competitive advantage feels most defensible?

activities.name: constraint canvas
activities.headline: create something brilliant with your hands tied
activities.content:
## **objective:**
challenge teams to solve a creative problem under imposed constraints, revealing how limitations can actually fuel more innovative thinking.
## **instructions:**
1. **frame the challenge**
1. **distribute briefs and constraints**
1. **ideation phase (12 min)**
1. **gallery walk (5 min)**
1. **debrief (5 min)**
## **materials:**
- paper and pens (1 set per team)
- sticky notes
- printed or written brief + constraint cards
- timer

activities.name: idea graveyard
activities.headline: resurrect your organisation's killed ideas — some may deserve a second life
activities.content:
## **objective:**
mine institutional knowledge by revisiting previously rejected innovation ideas and evaluating whether changed conditions make them viable today.
## **instructions:**
1. **excavation phase (8 min):**
1. **obituary phase (5 min):**
1. **resurrection phase (8 min):**
1. present your resurrected idea to the group.
## **materials:**
- sticky notes (tombstone-shaped if you're feeling dramatic)
- markers
- whiteboard or wall space
## **debrief questions:**
- why do organisations have trouble revisiting killed ideas?
- which resurrected idea has the most potential today?
- how do you create a culture that regularly revisits the graveyard?

activities.name: role reversal debate
activities.headline: argue the position you disagree with most
activities.content:
## **objective:**
strengthen perspective-taking and intellectual humility by requiring participants to construct the strongest possible argument for a position they disagree with.
## **instructions:**
1. **introduce the concept**
1. **choose a topic and take positions (3 min)**
1. **preparation phase (5 min)**
1. **structured debate (10 min)**
1. **debrief (5 min)**
## **materials:**
- paper and pens (for argument preparation)
- timer

activities.name: story spine
activities.headline: build a collaborative narrative one line at a time
activities.content:
## **objective:**
practice collaborative storytelling using a simple narrative framework, developing skills in building on others’ ideas, sequencing cause and effect, and finding meaning in shared creative work.
## **instructions:**
1. **teach the story spine**
1. **warm-up round (3 min)**
1. **small group round (10 min)**
1. **share and reflect (5 min)**

activities.name: nonviolent communication practice
activities.headline: learn the observation-feeling-need-request framework for conflict-free honesty
activities.content:
## **objective:**
learn and practise nonviolent communication (NVC) — a structured approach to expressing difficult truths without blame, judgement, or aggression.
## **instructions:**
1. introduce the **4 steps of NVC:**
1. **example transformation:**
1. **practice round 1 — observations (5 min):**
1. **practice round 2 — full NVC (10 min):**
1. **practice round 3 — receiving NVC (5 min):**
## **common traps:**
- "I feel that you..." — not a feeling, it's a thought
- "I feel attacked" — interpretation of intent, not an emotion
- "I need you to stop..." — a demand disguised as a need
## **debrief questions:**
- which step was hardest? why?
- how does separating observation from evaluation change the conversation?
- where could you use NVC this week?

activities.name: the difficult conversation rehearsal
activities.headline: script and practise a real conversation before you have it for real
activities.content:
## **objective:**
build confidence for real difficult conversations by scripting key messages, rehearsing delivery, and receiving coaching in a safe environment.
## **instructions:**
1. each participant identifies a **real difficult conversation** they need to have (or a realistic hypothetical).
1. **scripting phase (5 min):** write answers to:
1. **rehearsal phase (15 min):** work in triads:
1. **round 1:** speaker delivers the conversation. pause after 3 minutes.
1. **coach feedback:** what landed well? where did the speaker lose confidence or clarity?
1. **round 2:** speaker tries again, incorporating feedback.
1. rotate roles so everyone gets a turn.
## **coaching prompts for observers:**
- did the speaker lead with facts or emotions?
- was their body language congruent with their words?
- did they stay curious or get defensive?
- where was the strongest moment?
## **debrief questions:**
- what changed between round 1 and round 2?
- what surprised you about hearing yourself say the words out loud?
- how will you prepare for the real conversation?

activities.name: customer safari interview
activities.headline: go talk to real humans — bring back insights in 15 minutes
activities.content:
## **objective:**
practice human-centred design research by conducting rapid guerrilla interviews with real people, then synthesising insights into actionable design directions.
## **instructions:**
1. define the problem space (e.g., "morning routines," "workplace collaboration," "public transport").
1. each team prepares **5 open-ended questions** — no yes/no questions allowed:
1. teams leave the room and find **3 people** to interview (5 min each).
1. return to base. **synthesis phase (10 min):**
1. present findings to the group.
## **materials:**
- prepared question templates
- notebooks or phones for notes
- sticky notes and markers for synthesis
## **debrief questions:**
- what did you learn that you didn't expect?
- how did real responses differ from your assumptions?
- what would you investigate further with more time?

activities.name: threshold concept gallery
activities.headline: map the ideas that transformed your understanding and share the struggle of getting there
activities.content:
## **objective:**
identify and share the transformative ideas that permanently changed your understanding of a subject, exploring the difficult journey of grasping concepts that initially seem impossible.
## **instructions:**
1. introduce **threshold concepts** — ideas that:
1. **mapping phase (8 min):** each person creates a **threshold concept poster** including:
1. **gallery walk (7 min):** post all posters. everyone walks around reading them silently. leave sticky note comments or questions on others' posters.
1. **discussion (8 min):**
## **materials:**
- large paper or poster-sized sheets
- markers
- sticky notes for gallery walk comments
## **debrief questions:**
- why are the most important ideas often the hardest to grasp?
- how do you support someone who's in the liminal space?
- what threshold concept do you wish everyone in your field understood?

activities.name: fishbowl dialogue
activities.headline: step into the centre and think out loud
activities.content:
## **objective:**
create a structured space for deep dialogue where a small group discusses openly while the rest observes, then rotates — building empathy, active listening, and the courage to think in public.
## **instructions:**
1. **set up the fishbowl**
1. **introduce the topic**
1. **round 1 (8 min)**
1. **silent pause (1 min)**
1. **rotation**
1. **round 2 (8 min)**
1. **whole group debrief (5 min)**

activities.name: the apology lab
activities.headline: practise the anatomy of a genuine apology before you need one
activities.content:
## **objective:**
learn the components of an effective apology and practise delivering one in a low-stakes environment.
## **instructions:**
1. introduce the **5 parts of a real apology:**
1. give each pair a **scenario card** with a mild workplace offence:
1. person A delivers a **bad apology** first ("sorry if you were offended"). discuss why it fails.
1. person A delivers a **good apology** using all 5 parts. person B gives feedback.
1. swap roles with a new scenario.
## **common bad apology patterns to avoid:**
- "I'm sorry you feel that way" (deflection)
- "I'm sorry but..." (excuse)
- "mistakes were made" (passive voice)
- "I already said sorry" (impatience)
## **debrief questions:**
- which part of the apology was hardest to deliver?
- what's the difference between a good apology and a bad one?
- why do genuine apologies feel so difficult?
